speaker: interviewee
text: --that he's a-- a POW. And he looked around-- didn't talk too much. And he said, don't move. Stay here. And he left. And I don't know if it was a 1/2 or an hour later-- he walked in to us and was carrying two kilograms-- it's almost like five pound-- loaf of fresh bread and a [NON-ENGLISH]-- a whole package of margarine--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm!

speaker: interviewee
text: --like a pound. And he gave it to us. And we looked at him, and we didn't believe it-- a whole bread, and we can eat as much as we want.

speaker: interviewer
text: So of course we sat down and we started. And we thanked him. And then he ask us where we slept. We said here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he made this-- his head-- and he didn't like it. And he went out again, and he came back and brought us straw, so we wouldn't sleep on the naked floor. And then he left, and we never saw him again. He probably left the village and everything. But I will always remember his kindness, because he really doesn't know what it meant to us that here is such a good person giving us our life, because bread meant life. But we didn't eat as much as we wanted-- believe it or not. Because we didn't know that next day the war will be over. And the Germans, we learned, they didn't give us food, even though they had.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then we had the bread. We stayed inside. And then Irene was quiet too. And we slept another night.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the next morning was opposite of the first morning. We looked out, and there was not a soul outside. You could touch the stillness. It was so quiet.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, Irene, what happened? Did every-- the-- the earth swallow everybody? We didn't know what to do. So I opened the door. I was curious. And I went out-- and nothing. And we stood outside. And suddenly, I heard-- we started to hear a noise.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, we were in the war for five years, we-- and it came closer. And we recognized the noise of tanks. And even though, tanks-- and I didn't know what it was. I stood-- it was quite far. But when they came close enough that we saw it was tanks-- we saw tanks coming, and on the two sides, soldiers walking parallel with the tanks-- you can see this on the movies-- in war movies. And they had their rifles and the bayonets on the top. And the tanks were coming very slowly, and the soldiers with them. And suddenly, I recognize that they are Russians. And I start to scream. Irene, the Russians are coming! And we ran back from excitement to the little room. And we cried. And we hugged. And we kissed, and we said, the war is over. We are free! And we didn't know what to do. And we were just standing and crying there. And suddenly, I open the door, and while I was-- wanted to go out, there was standing a Russian officer. He was six feet tall, but I looked up at him, and I said, he's 100 feet tall-- beautiful-- a man in a uniform that is friendly to me-- not a German uniform. And I looked at him. And I said three words to him. And later I told my husband, and he was laughing. The three words were in three languages. I said, [NON-ENGLISH]-- Czech, Polish, and Russian. But the keyword was [NON-ENGLISH]. And when I told him [NON-ENGLISH], he looked at me--

speaker: interviewer
text: Hebrew, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it very-- yeah, Hebrew, but in Russian you can say [NON-ENGLISH], or you say [NON-ENGLISH]. And I said [NON-ENGLISH]. And he looked at me, and he-- his answer was [NON-ENGLISH]? Do you speak Yiddish? And I said, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he started. He said, how come you're alive? He started right away to talk to me Yiddish. And for a 1/2 an hour he bombarded me with questions. How come I am alive? And-- and he thought there are no Jews alive. And I started to tell-- and what is my name? And I said Edith. He couldn't pronounce it. Ethel-- he called me Ethel. Ethel. I said, call me Ethel. And he talked and talked.

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS] And he said, you know, Ethel, I had a sister. How old I am-- he asked me all-- anything possible to ask at that moment. And I told him, I'm 17. He said, you know, I had a little sister your age, and she was killed by the Germans. And I am coming from Kiev. And first he said to me, my name is Avrum Avramele. This is how they called him-- Avramele. And he said, I am a doctor-- medical doctor, and I am a captain in the Russian army.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was the first person who gave me back my freedom, my life. He was a Jew in the Russian army, an officer, and he was so kind. We talked for about a 1/2 an hour. I don't really know the time, I'm just assuming. And he couldn't get away from me. He was-- I think he was maybe even more happy to see me than I was-- no, it's not possible-- than I was happy to see him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he start to look in his pockets to give me something. And suddenly, he took out a piece of soap. He said, Ethel, I want to give you something. I have to go because they are waiting for me-- I have to go with my unit. And I have nothing, just this piece of soap. Please take it. And I took the soap. And I said, if he would know for a whole year I didn't have soap-- and then we used the soap. And I saved a little piece of soap. This is the only thing that I still have left-- the only thing I have from that time. And it's 50 years old. It will be 50 years old May 8, 194-- '95-- 50 years old. And I have this little piece that I-- I keep it. And this reminds me of Avramele. And I will give it to my children so they can keep it. And I would give-- I don't know what I would give today if I would know his-- his address, his name, and-- and to talk to him, and to tell him what-- what he meant for me. And this is how my liberation came back. And then the Russians told the Germans-- we moved in in the houses-- they should give us whatever we want-- clothes, food. And if I will-- I cannot begin to tell you when we moved into the houses. We found so much food-- salamis and all kind of cold cuts. This was a-- a agricultural village.

speaker: interviewer
text: We found so much food that I-- after 50 years, I still can't understand why they didn't give us a piece of bread-- those German women-- when they knew that the Russians are coming. They knew they will take away from them everything. Why couldn't they show a little kindness to give those hungry girls a bite of bread?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then after the war, wherever you went, they said, I didn't do it. Hitler did it. They all were clean-- lily clean-- lily white clean. No, they were not. And when people ask me how I feel for the Germans, this is how I remember them-- that the last day of the war, they didn't want to help. And they didn't risk anything. It wasn't that they risked life. If some people save Jewish lives, they risk their own life. They didn't risk anything. They just had to be like this last woman who gave me where to-- to sleep, or this French POW who gave us the bread. Nothing happened to them. And this is how they were. So I cannot, never ever get over it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Edith, we-- we-- we do want to hear how it was for you after the war. We have very little time, so tell us. You wanted-- did you ever go back to your village to look for your people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us how you found them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. First, I stayed two weeks in this village to get-- to gain some weight, to-- to get a little strength. We drank a lot of milk. I went-- every day they had to-- they-- I ask from them a bucket of milk-- whatever I ask. I moved in-- in-- in-- in the-- I must tell you this-- moved in in the house. And I went in a normal bed to sleep-- with pillows and bits of cover. And the next morning, I-- I woke up. I want to get off bed, and I couldn't. I collapsed. And I tried 10 times to stand on my feet, and I couldn't. And then I had to crawl back-- my feet didn't work. I crawled back in bed. And I realized that my feet were telling me something. They told me, I kept you going from three weeks. I need a rest. Now, you stay in bed, because we need a rest. And I stayed in bed. And then when I was stronger, I got up and got out. And then the girls from the forest came in, and everybody was clean, in new clothes. It was like a changed-- a new world for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I stayed there two weeks. Then I went back to-- back to life. First, we came to Prague, then to Budapest.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Budapest, in every place we had hotels. We were called refugees. And especially with the number, I had open doors everywhere. I got a-- a where to sleep in hotels. We got food-- even pocket money. And then I went to Budapest, where I heard that my mother is alive. And first I didn't believe it. Then I start to go home. Trains were free, and-- but you never knew where-- you hopped on a train, and you never knew which direction [LAUGHS] it will take you. But finally, after two weeks, I reached-- usually it took-- take 7, 8, 10 hours. It took two weeks. I found my mother. And we-- we slept the first night together. We-- we hugged each other to-- to-- to be sure that we are alive. Next day, my sister came home. But we didn't stay too long there. We didn't want, because the Russians were there, and we wanted to leave Europe. And we were afraid if we stay under the Russians, we won't be able to get out. Even though I have very-- my feelings about the Russians are good, because I was liberated by them. My husband was in the Russian army. My mother was liberated by them, my sister. So I feel grateful to them. And I don't-- I'm not going into politics with the Russians. And then we went back to Czechoslovakia. And we couldn't stay there either. And then we left and went, from all places, back to Germany in a DP camp-- displaced persons camp-- in Heidenheim near-- near Leipzig-- no-- I'm sorry-- near Stuttgart-- a camp. And there are people from all over Europe. Jewish people came. And we were living there. We stayed there for 2 and 1/2 years. We could have gone to America right away, because I had an uncle who sent all the children in the family who were survived to send an affidavit. But we had to wait for a quota. And I met Michael. And he loved me very much. And he wouldn't change me for anything in the world. Only one thing mattered-- he didn't want to go-- only to Israel, to Palestine. He said, after all we went through, this is the only place to go. And then we decided instead of America, we had to leave. We went with the last Aliyah Bet. We went to Israel. And there, the minute we arrived, he was drafted to the army. After he went to World War II, he lost all his family-- he will tell you about it himself. He was drafted, but the Independence War ended. And then the second war, he was drafted when it was in '56-- 1956, the Suez Canal War. By then I had my two daughters. Esther was seven years old. Shula was a baby. And one day, he was drafted and had to leave. And it was for two weeks I didn't know if he's alive or not. And then when he came back, he told us he was at the Suez Canal where Nasser threatened that he will throw the Jews into the sea. So one side was this-- was Egypt. And the other side was the sea. And he got so scared. And I could tell you a million other stories, but the honest truth is he got--

speaker: interviewer
text: He was so scared.

speaker: interviewee
text: --he got so scared to lose his new family, that he had the urge to-- he wanted to come to the United States. And this is how it happened that we came here in '59.

speaker: interviewer
text: So our children are born there. And he came here right away. We are very devoted to Israel-- it's our country. We went back as often as we could. And I was going every year while my mother was alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS] And now I am here. My two daughters are married-- well, one was married. One is married. We have four grandchildren they're our-- our life. And if they would go back to Israel, I would-- would go with them. We want to be together with the children. So this is-- this is our life till now.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSESTHER SINGER FOR 5 SECONDS] Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: [HEBREW] [SINGING IN HEBREW]

speaker: interviewee
text: Joni, do you want to sing a song? No? No. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: [SINGING IN HEBREW]

speaker: interviewee
text: You don't want to? OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SINGING HEBREW]

speaker: interviewee
text: [HEBREW]

speaker: interviewee
text: [SINGING HEBREW]

speaker: interviewee
text: [SINGING IN HEBREW]

speaker: interviewee
text: [SINGING IN HEBREW] [HEBREW]

speaker: interviewee
text: [HEBREW]

speaker: interviewee
text: [HEBREW]

speaker: interviewer
text: Come sit down. Come sit down.

speaker: interviewee
text: Who, I?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you, Edith, would you introduce your family, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is Michael, my husband and my best friend.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long have you been married?

speaker: interviewee
text: A birthday. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: Birthday. From 48.

speaker: interviewer
text: From 48.

speaker: interviewee
text: 46 years-- 46 and 1/2.

speaker: interviewee
text: 46 Years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is next to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Next to him, this is Eve Havale, Shula's daughter. Shula is my younger daughter. And this is her son, Joni-- Jonathan. He's two years old. Havale had yesterday, birthday. She was five. And this is my older daughter Esther. And her two children are not here, but we will give you some pictures. Her older-- her daughter's name is Yael-- Yael Shira-- and her son's name is Jishai-- Jishai Meier. And Shula's husband is not here either, and his name is Uri Arbel. This is my immediate family, my most important people in my life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd just like to ask either one of you how having parents who went through the Holocaust have impacted your life. How do you think it made your life different, having had parents that went through the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: For me, it's hard to answer, because those are the only parents I've had, so I don't know another way of life. But as I get older and I see different styles of-- different lifestyles, I realize that our home was different, and it was special. We're very close, which I've noticed among other friends who are from the same kind of background. We take care of each other. Maybe sometimes we worry too much.

speaker: interviewer
text: My first memories of the Holocaust are of the stories-- is-- my earliest memory is when I was very, very young. I remember visiting a friend. And the-- the mother was wearing a short-sleeved shirt. And I asked the-- the other little girl, how come your mother doesn't have a number? Because I was born into it, I just assumed all mothers have a tattoo number on their forearm. And it was quite a few years before I realized it-- it wasn't an automatic.

speaker: interviewee
text: I never knew this.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had the opposite experience. I was always aware that we were different-- that I was different, that my mother was different. And I suffered in a sense from it. We came to America, so it was the combination of the new immigrant experience, plus I went to a public school with all American kids-- there was no one else like me. And I was embarrassed and ashamed sometimes. And I felt a personal pain. And the opposite happened in the sense that as I got older, I came to accept it. And for me, it was-- it was every part of my life. I can't separate it from any part of my life. It-- it permeates everything I do, or think, or feel to this day, especially as a parent now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, Now we're going to show some photographs. Thank you for coming.

speaker: interviewee
text: Would you want to say something, Havale?

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you want to say something too, Havale?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wish they-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: About [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a postcard from Chust, where I was born and I grew up. And it's very typical. This mountain was-- has a story to it. And I think every person who lived or was once in Chust will see this postcard, will recognize it. After the war, when I went back, I had a feeling I will never go back again. And I bought all the postcards that were available in the store. So this is why I want to start with this. This is my family picture, the only picture I have from back home. My parents-- no. The first person is Lilly, my sister. She was the oldest. Next is my mother, Frieda. In the middle is my brother, Yanku. This is my father, Shabtai Samuel. And on his laps, the little girl is me. And this picture was taken in 1934 or '35. So I was probably five or six years old-- not more. And this picture is very precious to me, because I don't have any other pictures from my brother or my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Chust, at home. In those days, it was customary to make family pictures, and, of course, everything was black and white. And-- [PAUSESTHER SINGER FOR 3 SECONDS] --I don't remember where it was taken-- but at home, in Chust. This is one of my school pictures. Somehow, I found-- when we left Chust, I hid some things. And one of them were the school pictures. This is taken in 1930-- I don't know which year-- like fourth grade-- in the Czech school. It was still Czechoslovakia in the fourth grade. In the middle, the lady, is my teacher. I don't remember her name anymore. Most of the children are Jewish. There's only one boy who is not Jewish. He lost his hand. He was once climbing on an electric pole, and because he wasn't so presentable-- so they put him in a Jewish class. So he's the only boy who is not Jewish. And you can see all the boys without hat. They were not allowed to wear a hat or a kippah in the class, because it was a non-Jewish school-- a public school. And I am to the left of the teacher-- the second person to the left. I have a flower in my hand and a bow in my hair. My mother always gave me a flower when we took school pictures to-- to look better. So I must be here about 10 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Let me see the girl with that. OK. This is a picture from Heidenheim, the displaced person's camp where I was after the war. We arrived there early '46, and this was in the summer of '46. I joined the Betar Movement, the movement that wanted to fight to free Israel. I am on the right, the first one on the right, kneeling. We had uniforms-- white blouses and blue skirts with a tie. And we had leaders, and we used to listen to lectures, and to sing a lot. We really didn't do any fighting or any-- any practicing to fight, just to sing and-- and be together, and plan for the future. This is our wedding picture, Michael and-- this is our wedding picture. We got married, from all places in the world, in Munich, in 1948, May 27. And we're still married since then, [NON-ENGLISH]-- 46 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me the name of these people. I know that's you and--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Michael, Michael Singer. And my name is Edith. It was Edith Slomovits. Now it's Singer, too, for more years than I-- I remember. And we had a very small wedding-- just a few people. And-- and we went for a honeymoon after this. And we live happily ever after. We don't talk about problems.

speaker: interviewer
text: Those pictures are ready. Sh!

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother, Frieda Slomovits. This picture was taken in Israel, about 1975. She lived in Israel. After the war, we went there. She lived alone till the last few years of her life. The last-- she lived with my sister for a few years. And then she was in a old age home the last year and a 1/2 before she died. And she died in her sleep in 1981, a week before Pesach. She died in her sleep. And we're very grateful for-- to God that she didn't-- she wasn't sick, and she didn't suffer. She had her life, and she went peacefully. And we remember her always in her good mood and good health.

speaker: interviewer
text: There are your parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, oh, oh. OK. You're right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sh.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my older daughter, Esther, at her wedding day. We're take-- escorting her to the chuppah. It happened on December 22, 1970. My first child getting married was a very, very exciting day.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my younger daughter Shula, and her groom, Uri Arbel. They got married in August 30, 1988. And our excitement was all over again. And it was another very happy day in our lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: Here was this made--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Yael, Yael Shira Ovid, my oldest grandchild.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yael!

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in '92. She will be next month, 17 years old. And because she was my first grandchild, it was a very, very special event in my life. My whole life changed when she arrived. And I will be always grateful to have her for it, because I became a grandmother the first time. [BACKGROUND NOISE] This is Jishai Meier Ovid, my first grandson, who's also special in his own way, because he is to me like a son whom I never had; with his grandfather, Michael, at his bar mitzvah. And we were very happy to have this little celebration. We didn't have a big bar mitzvah. It was in our house. And it was very, very special for us. This is Yael and Jishai in 1985. It was a very special evening in our family-- my husband was honored. Michael was honored in his school where he worked for-- he still works for so many years in Hebrew high school. And they made a HaMotzi at the dinner-- Jishai and Yael. This is a silver ring that was given to us by a friend who was in Dachau. And he was a jeweler, and he worked with jewelry. And this-- he told a story that he made this ring from keys that was pulled out by prisoners, Jewish prisoners, before they fought and after they-- they were killed. And one day when nobody was looking, he made himself this ring with his number. And it says Dachau on it. And this friend doesn't live anymore, so I keep it as a memory of him and of the Holocaust. This is a dreidel that's very special to me. I don't remember anymore how I found it. But when I was back in Chust, I found a few things. This was one of them-- a dreidel that my brother made. The boys in Europe used-- before Hanukkah-- used to take out lead from pencils-- because they didn't have other lead-- and melt it. And then they made the dreidel. And he was very handy with many things with his hands, so he made once this dreidel and all the letters on it. And I keep it. And every Hanukkah, we take it out just to look at it, because it's the only thing I have left from my brother, whom I loved very, very much. [PAUSESTHER SINGER FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my family in 1932. To the left, this little boy is my young brother, [NON-ENGLISH], seven years old; then my stepmother [NON-ENGLISH] my father Wolf, myself. In the back to the left is my sister, [NON-ENGLISH] and my older sister, [NON-ENGLISH]. Next to my stepmother and father is our youngest child, my youngest sister, [NON-ENGLISH]. Except me-- I'm still alive. They all-- they all were killed by the Nazis--

speaker: interviewer
text: What is their last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: --in 1942. The last name-- Singer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what city was it in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sarny, which is in Volhynia, in the western Ukraine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you are the only one that survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm the only who survived, because I was drafted into the Russian army in 1941, before the war. And luckily, I survived. So I'm here. They all perished.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH].
